featuredQuotes.text: My son is finishing fast to just play games like prodigy . He doesn’t work of handwriting at all!! Bring play back

My elementary children have repeatedly been exposed to inappropriate content on their school issued Chromebooks. They are often on websites that we do not allow at home. I am concerned how the screen time at school is affecting them academically.
I believe devices create a distraction and double the work for teachers.
Shows and movies being played without parents consent on screens very frequently.
I’d like to be able to access my child’s screen time per week and history

Screens seem to be used as a babysitter. My child in 4th grade did not get to play on the playground from Christmas break to the end of March because the teachers thought it was too cold and they had them sit inside on screens instead (not technically too cold to be outside per policy)
My 1st grader was shown an inappropriate image that scared her by another student in her class on his Chromebook.

Knox County District 2 Parent.text:
Excessive screen time reducing physical activity and face-to-face interaction
Privacy and data security concerns involving student information
Cyberbullying and inappropriate online behavior among students
Overreliance on technology limiting development of critical thinking and handwriting skills
Exposure to inappropriate or unreliable online…

Data consistently shows that children learn more effectively when they are not on tech. I do not want my child using screens at school and I ESPECIALLY do not want my children having any contact with generative AI. I am ready to pull my children from KCS over this issue.
My biggest concern is that Chromebooks get used in place of peer interactions (like stations) Time where they can be working together independently but instead spend that time doing iready lessons.
We have a huge amount of concern about all the aspects listed above. The day is excessive for Kindergarten students already and they need movement and hands on time and recess. Not screens.
It seems to vary teachers to teacher how much time they have on computers. Like different teacher to teacher and that’s very frustrating to me.

Knox County District 5 Parent.text:
These devices are not a useful way of learning. The kids are tested too much as it is. 

Specific issues we’ve encountered last year:
1. A child never listened to the teacher and kept sneaking his Chromebook when it was time to focus on the teacher and self-guided work. This was a major distraction for my child. 
2.

We do not believe there is a filter or firewall protecting students from accessing the entire internet including inappropriate content. We have direct experience with students in our child’s class accessing material on a school provided chrome that was pornographic in nature and would be blocked with any reasonable firewall.

We are okay with some screen time & use screens at home as well. However, we are deeply concerned on testing on screens. Our children are performing significantly lower on screen testing than traditional methods. Their teachers have reassured us to not worry and that our children are on target.

Our 3rd grader was constantly on the Chromebook. All he came home talking about was different games he played on the computer and begging for us to get those games at home. It was a constant battle at home. We noticed he struggled more than ever to go outside and play and use his imagination.

The constant screen time at school has significantly impacted my high schoolers ability to do “boring” non-screen tasks that do not flood his brain with dopamine and impacted his ability to engage socially with peers. Adolescents are struggling to launch after high school due to this.

Kids play academic games and rush through coursework to get to the reward. Spelling is atrocious when having to use pen and paper because teachers have them write essays on Google Docs so they turn on spellcheck and don’t pay attention to what actually needs to be fixed.

Knox County Parent.text: Middle school uses the chromebooks all day long in every classroom. In every class the access to the assignments or tasks for the day is through Canvas and the Chromebook. The ELA curriculum is entirely on the device and students are reading excerpts from a book (but never the entire novel) on a screen.

I have taught in Knox county for twenty years.  I was a math teacher at West Valley Middle.  I have seen the dramatic decrease of the students ability to learn as we were the first school to implement the 1:1 initiative.  It’s been detrimental and each year I saw my students performing at lower and lower levels.

Most of the school work in grades 6-8 has been done on Chromebook. It should NOT be a replacement for work done on pencil and paper and it is difficult for parents to see student work once it us submitted. It's hard to know what my kid needs to work on if I am unable to see his work.

There is just no reason that such young age children all need their own chrome book. It is very well established how bad screen time is for our children so I don’t know why we are providing such opportunities to our young elementary grades.

We are told from a young age that screen time hurts them. I was horrified when indound out my Kindergartener waa going to be given a screen. Why? 
It take the content control from the parents. It is not what school should be about.
Concerned about what filters Knox Co uses as my my child (and others I’ve come to find out after sharing my experience) have seen inappropriate things

Knox County District 4 Parent.text: My 6th (now 7th) grader brought his chromebook home every day. SO heavy in his bag. He was able to go to YouTube and would do that in class and at home whenever he could. We have strict rules at home but it was hard to enforce when another device is in the house and when he had homework on that device many nights.

I am very concerned about screens during indoor recess/finishing early  and screens for math skills, like iReady. Current research shows those programs are not beneficial for student's’ learning, thus a time filler and way for kids who need more challenge to progress.

One of my primary concerns is student access to the internet through school-issued Chromebooks. While I understand the educational benefits of technology, I believe there should be strong filtering systems and ongoing monitoring in place to help ensure students are not inadvertently exposed to inappropriate content.

I would love to see more meaningful no -screen work offered for early finishers or when there is extra time in the classroom. Ongoing projects and enrichment that pique their interests that can be worked on in the fringe moments for example. I think technology at this age has more potential for harm than good.

I feel like especially at the elementary and middle school levels we need a shift back to more hands on learning. Not only are my children on computers all day at school, they come home with computer lessons to do as homework as well. It is unnecessary and being overused throughout the school systems.

Device use in schools is just part of the issue. Most kids rely on their cell phones/gaming screens after school hours so we are talking about compounded exposure to screens. Society has revved up the relevance of technology so that children are being influenced to think they need devices in every setting, to benefit whom, we sometimes are unsure.

Knox County District 8 Parent.text: I know adults use devices at work every day. It is also our responsibility as adults to teach children how to use these devices for work and educational means. This does not need to begin until after age twelve to thirteen. There is no reason for a toddler or young child to need screen time.
I know several examples where students at a young ages have seen explicit content at school. Not at our school but another school. This is unacceptable.

Knox County District 1 Parent.text: Specifically for elementary school the screen time is too much. My son had about an hour of just computer time a day, in addition to screen use for instruction, practice and tests. During this hour he was working alone on either math or ELA but often that time could be spent watching videos or playing a high dopamine reward game.
While technology certainly has a place in education, I believe the current level of screen use is becoming a hindrance to meaningful learning and development for my girls who are in K and 2nd.

Knox County District 6 Parent.text: The fact that children can access anything on YouTube and other websites as there are no blockers and if there are they are able to find ways around them, which they should not be able to. They should only have access to academic resources and sites and the county can make sure of that and flag/block anything else.
I know of multiple elementary school kids that have been able to access inappropriate games (such as Five Nights with Epstein) during class time.
My son is finishing fast to just play games like prodigy . He doesn’t work of handwriting at all!! Bring play back

Knox County District 7 Parent.text: My child retains better with hands on learning. Data shows this to be true across the board for all learners. Technology should be in addition to a highly qualified lesson, not the main lesson itself.
I just know my children are spending more time on their devices than necessary for a learning tool. They have told me stories about watching Nerf videos on YouTube. They have way too much time.

Knox County District 3 Parent.text: We’re too addicted to screens as it is, I’d prefer it not be used to replace teaching time or as a reward.
It is not developmentally appropriate for elementary age students to be on the screens for the amount of time they are.
Screens create regression and lacks to diversify processing, it builds intolerance around patience and uncertainty.

Knox County District 9 Parent.text: Fact that teachers need more support/break and technology seems to be the answer. Wish it could be outside breaks instead. Use of courtyards etc.

District 2.text:
Excessive screen time reducing physical activity and face-to-face interaction
Privacy and data security concerns involving student information
Cyberbullying and inappropriate online behavior among students
Overreliance on technology limiting development of critical thinking and handwriting skills
Exposure to inappropriate or unreliable online…

Data consistently shows that children learn more effectively when they are not on tech. I do not want my child using screens at school and I ESPECIALLY do not want my children having any contact with generative AI. I am ready to pull my children from KCS over this issue.
My biggest concern is that Chromebooks get used in place of peer interactions (like stations) Time where they can be working together independently but instead spend that time doing iready lessons.
We have a huge amount of concern about all the aspects listed above. The day is excessive for Kindergarten students already and they need movement and hands on time and recess. Not screens.
It seems to vary teachers to teacher how much time they have on computers. Like different teacher to teacher and that’s very frustrating to me.

District 5.text:
These devices are not a useful way of learning. The kids are tested too much as it is. 

Specific issues we’ve encountered last year:
1. A child never listened to the teacher and kept sneaking his Chromebook when it was time to focus on the teacher and self-guided work. This was a major distraction for my child. 
2.

We do not believe there is a filter or firewall protecting students from accessing the entire internet including inappropriate content. We have direct experience with students in our child’s class accessing material on a school provided chrome that was pornographic in nature and would be blocked with any reasonable firewall.

We are okay with some screen time & use screens at home as well. However, we are deeply concerned on testing on screens. Our children are performing significantly lower on screen testing than traditional methods. Their teachers have reassured us to not worry and that our children are on target.

Our 3rd grader was constantly on the Chromebook. All he came home talking about was different games he played on the computer and begging for us to get those games at home. It was a constant battle at home. We noticed he struggled more than ever to go outside and play and use his imagination.

The constant screen time at school has significantly impacted my high schoolers ability to do “boring” non-screen tasks that do not flood his brain with dopamine and impacted his ability to engage socially with peers. Adolescents are struggling to launch after high school due to this.

Kids play academic games and rush through coursework to get to the reward. Spelling is atrocious when having to use pen and paper because teachers have them write essays on Google Docs so they turn on spellcheck and don’t pay attention to what actually needs to be fixed.

I am increasingly concerned about the amount of screen exposure children are receiving during the school day. In many cases, it appears to exceed the limits that many parents, including myself, would allow at home.

Minimal amount of actual instructional time from the teachers in every grade starting at first grade for my oldest son who will be starting 4th grade in the fall. Way more time on “learning games” than face to face teaching time. All he talks about when he gets home from school are the games he plays in the Chromebook.

Minimal amount of actual instructional time from the teachers in every grade starting at first grade for my oldest son who will be starting 4th grade in the fall. Way more time on “learning games” than face to face teaching time. All he talks about when he gets home from school are the games he plays in the Chromebook.

The amount of screen time my children receive at school is more than I even allow at home.

They are given screen time during snack breaks instead of being encouraged to socialize with their classmates and develop important communication skills. They are also exposed to a variety of shows and videos that I would not approve of at home.

It is my belief that there has been an overreliance on and overindulgence in the use of technology on the part of adults in our elementary school. I also believe faculty are not communicating with one another about the use of screens at different points in the school day, so nobody is fully aware of how much screens are being utilized across the entire day.

My kids, that LOVE devices, are OVER the amount of time they are used in school. My rising 4th grader has complained since Kindergarten. He often complains that he doesn’t want to go to school because ‘we just spend all day on our computer’.

Children have been given their screens as an option during indoor recess and rather than choosing to play with friends, play games, read books etc my children have shared that most children opt to play on their Chrimebooks. Similarly they have shared that they get to be on their Chromebook if they finish their work early

My child had direct experience that the school firewalls do not exist.  A third grade student was caught viewing pornographic materials on a school provided Chromebook.  This should have been caught by a firewall if there was one.
It has been proven that pen to paper writing increases retention.  Seems backwards that we would encourage the opposite.
A brain break or recess should not be staring at a screen! It should be movement and interaction.
It’s too much. Our kids are in school to learn hands on from a person, not a device.

District 4.text: My 6th (now 7th) grader brought his chromebook home every day. SO heavy in his bag. He was able to go to YouTube and would do that in class and at home whenever he could. We have strict rules at home but it was hard to enforce when another device is in the house and when he had homework on that device many nights.

I am very concerned about screens during indoor recess/finishing early  and screens for math skills, like iReady. Current research shows those programs are not beneficial for student's’ learning, thus a time filler and way for kids who need more challenge to progress.

One of my primary concerns is student access to the internet through school-issued Chromebooks. While I understand the educational benefits of technology, I believe there should be strong filtering systems and ongoing monitoring in place to help ensure students are not inadvertently exposed to inappropriate content.

I would love to see more meaningful no -screen work offered for early finishers or when there is extra time in the classroom. Ongoing projects and enrichment that pique their interests that can be worked on in the fringe moments for example. I think technology at this age has more potential for harm than good.

I feel like especially at the elementary and middle school levels we need a shift back to more hands on learning. Not only are my children on computers all day at school, they come home with computer lessons to do as homework as well. It is unnecessary and being overused throughout the school systems.

Device use in schools is just part of the issue. Most kids rely on their cell phones/gaming screens after school hours so we are talking about compounded exposure to screens. Society has revved up the relevance of technology so that children are being influenced to think they need devices in every setting, to benefit whom, we sometimes are unsure.

Screen Time and the use of devices are obviously a part of our world and climate today. I feel that children will get plenty of this outside of school hours, and it would be nice to see a decrease in the amount of one-on-one device use, and perhaps removal of technology the K-5 environment.

In our school, any children who are not in tier 2 or 3 small learning groups, are using chrome books to work on independent learning during this time. I do not believe this is the best use of time for my children who are not currently able to focus independently on productive work time.

There have been whisperings of Tcap testing being completely computerized next year. As a parent of a rising 3rd grader who struggles with test taking anxiety, my concern is that standardized testing on computers penalizes students for typing speed rather than content mastery.

I worry that my daughter’s learning experience is less impactful on devices. Watching lessons or referencing content online doesn’t seem to support her learning and I’m concerned the lack of book/paper/pencil work undermines her ability to practice and build good study skills.

The screens seem to be a band-aid for the help teachers have been needing since way before 2020. We just want more time with our amazing teachers or other wonderful personnel. And no more time with unnecessary games and videos that are not even allowed at our home.

Screen time was used for indoor recess, “brain breaks”, centers- virtually all day in different capacities. This includes shows/videos that the teacher would play. It was also an incentive for good behavior.

My kids have very limited screen access at home and so when they have the opportunity to use at school they jump at the chance and rush through their work to get to them, choose them during indoor recess, and miss out on peer to peer connection and  the beauty of boredom and creativity bc of them

My children were both able to access the adult YouTube at school. We do not allow them to watch YouTube at home and monitor all screen use strictly. They also use Chromebooks for indoor recess and centers. There is also no communication about tv shows that are shown and how often- and tv shows were shown very frequently.

Don’t want my children bringing home devices when we’ve purposefully kept them from unsupervised use of screens. Or children come home knowing lingo from viral videos and other content with zero educational value. Prefer zero screen usage for homework…paper and pen in all grades. Prefer hands-on skills and activities to any screen related content.

Screens seem to be used as a babysitter. My child in 4th grade did not get to play on the playground from Christmas break to the end of March because the teachers thought it was too cold and they had them sit inside on screens instead (not technically too cold to be outside per policy)

I am honestly not sure how much my child is on a screen but I do know they take brain break to play games online and I don't think that actually gives their brains a break. They watch movies and YouTube and even a classmate was exposed to porn by googling a sexual term on wikipedia.

It doesn’t matter how many safety procedures are in place, kids can always ALWAYS learn how to override them - either on purpose or accidentally. My son is in 1st grade and his classmate was able to access YouTube (accidentally).
They are addictive, kids play non educational games on them all day. They cause too much distraction and do not add value.

I do NOT like the amount of Chromebook time used.
1- It’s more than my children are allowed at home
2- used as rewards 
3- replacing personal instruction and true hands on learning 
4- REALLY do not like when teachers give option to be on Chromebook during indoor recess … make them play a game or interact with peers

The data and research is clear that children do not retain information and learning from screens. Paperand pencils and books are far better for their developing brains and ability to retain information.

I am very nervous about my children being able to bring their Chromebook’s home in middle school. We as a family have very much on purpose not given them tablets or computers at home because we do not want them to have access to the internet.

As both a child of the 90s and a teacher who began teaching in the early 2010s, I’ve had the unique experience of seeing educational technology evolve from an occasional classroom enhancement into a daily, individual experience for students.
Please read the research and look at the data on the effect of screens on young children and adolescents. The data is clear. Screens do not belong in the hands of our children.

We receive a technology information sheet (or consent form?) at the beginning of the school year, but I feel like it’s more to “satisfy a requirement” than actually explain to families how technology will be used at school.

2nd grader constantly shared he played Prodigy in class and that we he would fly through iReady just to play prodigy. His very high math scores went down from 1st grade despite playing a “math” based game everyday

I value my child learning in person from his teacher rather than through a screen. I believe it’s very critical at his age to learn within the classroom involving his peers as opposed to a program on the chromebook.

The kids frequently use devices at school as free time when they finish their work early and when rainy and they can’t go out for recess. They should not be replacing physical activity and social interactions.

Ed Tech products like Xtra Math being used for practice, rather than interacting with the child's actual teacher in the classroom. YouTube videos being used to teach concepts, rather than the teacher teaching.
There’s a reason schools in Silicon Valley do not allow screens in the classroom. The people that created this technology know the negative effects that can have on the developing mind of a child.
The amount of time spent on screens at school is very alarming. Kids are on screens various times throughout the day instead of learning from a teacher. Tests should not be given on computers.
Not only is the Chromebook a screen but teaching is done on a screen. Kids are essentially watching a Power Point presentation all day long.
My concern is that use of Chromebooks has replaced indoor recess time that could be spent interacting/playing games with other children, using hands to draw/play with legos, or reading.
I think it is absurd we are testing children in Kindergarten on computers - and other grades.  But I was appalled this past year when my child was tested with a Chromebook.

While I understand that screens might need to be used for assessments, I strongly believe that screens should not be used for explicit teaching OR for recreational purposes (indoor recess, breaks, etc.)
Learning through screens has been proven ineffective especially for younger children who do not have a base without technology to build from.
My oldest child rushes through his work so he can use his Chromebook and play Prodigy. I wish that there wasn’t an incentive to finish work.
I understand that devices can play an important role in education. I worry about the brain breaks and indoor recess screen time.
My kindergarten should not be allowed to freely Google things without supervision for fun, like she was allowed in her class.
I believe devices create a distraction and double the work for teachers.
Our children have their entire adulthood to be stuck at a computer screen- let’s let them enjoy childhood and appreciation for traditional learning environments
I think this is one of the most important issues facing middle and elementary schools right now in Knox Co.
Shows and movies being played without parents consent on screens very frequently.
I’d like to be able to access my child’s screen time per week and history
